picks.name: Alphabet Inc.
picks.rationale: GOOGL is the highest-conviction carry-over from every prior session this week. Google Cloud revenue surged +63% YoY in Q1 2026 to deliver $109.9B in total revenue (+20% YoY) with a $460B cloud backlog, while Berkshire Hathaway added +204% to its GOOGL stake in Q1 — both institutional and fundamental signals are aligned. At a 31.6% operating margin and with Alphabet aggressively funding AI expansion with ~$60B in global debt raised, the platform monetization flywheel is accelerating. The macro backdrop (steepening curve, higher rates) is a mild headwind on multiples, but the growth rate more than compensates at current valuation.
picks.catalyst: Q2 2026 earnings with Google Cloud growth acceleration and further AI monetization evidence across Search and YouTube
picks.entryNote: Trading near 52-week highs after the Q1 blowout; forward P/E ~22x on 2026 consensus — reasonable for a company growing cloud at 63% YoY. GOOG also announced a $0.22/share dividend with ex-date June 8, adding income support.
picks.exitTrigger: Google Cloud growth decelerates below 40% YoY for two consecutive quarters, or DOJ antitrust remedy forces Search divestiture
picks.keyRisk: Antitrust ruling requiring structural remedy to Search monopoly, which would impair the core revenue engine

picks.name: Broadcom Inc.
picks.rationale: AVGO is the purest play on AI custom silicon and networking outside of NVDA, and critically, it sits at a different point in the value chain — custom ASICs and switching, not GPU training. Q1 FY2026 EPS of $2.05 beat the $1.88 estimate by 9%, with YoY EPS growth of 28%, and Zacks projects FY2027 sales +40.5% and earnings +39.5%. AVGO avoids NVDA's export control overhang on H20/Blackwell chips to China since its revenue is primarily from hyperscaler custom chip programs. Sector rotation briefing confirmed AI infrastructure spend (hyperscaler capex $485B+ in 2026) as the dominant structural tailwind.
picks.catalyst: Q2 FY2026 earnings with EPS consensus of $2.32 — expected sequential double-digit EPS expansion confirming AI ASIC ramp
picks.entryNote: AVGO closed ~$427 on May 15 (per Yahoo Finance data), trading near 52-week highs after its strong Q1 beat. Forward P/E ~28-30x is premium but justified by the growth runway; FCF yield remains among the highest in semis.
picks.exitTrigger: Loss of a major hyperscaler custom chip design win, or gross margin compression below 60% as AI ASIC mix shifts
picks.keyRisk: Hyperscaler capex pullback — if one or more of MSFT/AMZN/GOOG/META slows AI infrastructure spend, AVGO faces an immediate earnings revision downgrade cycle

picks.name: Meta Platforms Inc.
picks.rationale: META delivered the fastest quarterly revenue growth since 2021 in Q1 2026 at +33% YoY to $56.31B, beating consensus, with a jaw-dropping 81.8% gross margin and 41.3% operating margin — the best in mega-cap tech. Q2 2026 guidance of $58-61B implies continued ~25% growth. Both Berkshire Hathaway (Abel era) and Bill Ackman's Pershing Square initiated or hold META as a new position, providing strong smart money validation. In the stagflation regime, META's advertising-driven, asset-light FCF model is one of the few tech businesses that can withstand higher discount rates on earnings power alone.
picks.catalyst: Q2 2026 earnings report with guidance for continued +25% revenue growth and Llama/AI monetization progress
picks.entryNote: Returned as a high-conviction position after briefly being medium-conviction mid-week. Trading at ~19x forward P/E on 2026 consensus — arguably the cheapest mega-cap tech name given its growth rate. Revisions are strongly positive post-Q1.
picks.exitTrigger: Operating margin falls below 35% due to AI capex escalation (Reality Labs or Llama infrastructure), or regulatory ad-targeting restrictions in EU/US impair monetization
picks.keyRisk: AI capex escalation eroding operating margins materially — Meta has committed to $60-65B in 2026 capex

picks.name: Eli Lilly and Company
picks.rationale: LLY is the only mega-cap healthcare name that scores cleanly across growth, quality, and defensive characteristics simultaneously — a rare combination in a stagflation regime. Q1 2026 revenue came in at $19.799B beating consensus, with a 48.62% EBITDA margin reflecting exceptional pricing power in GLP-1. The stock trades near $1,005 on May 15 — well below its 52-week high of $1,133.95 — offering a re-entry at an attractive discount (~11% below ATH) after the UNH-led healthcare sector selloff dragged LLY lower. Beta of just 0.48 provides natural portfolio ballast in a high-volatility macro regime. GLP-1 global addressable market (obesity, diabetes, cardiovascular, Alzheimer's) remains the single largest secular growth story in pharma with a patent runway through ~2031.
picks.catalyst: Next earnings report August 5, 2026 — expected to show continued Zepbound/Mounjaro demand acceleration; donanemab Alzheimer's launch trajectory
picks.catalystWindow: August 5, 2026 (next 11 weeks) plus June FDA decisions on tirzepatide label expansions
picks.entryNote: Trading at ~$1,005, ~11% below the $1,133.95 ATH set in January 2026. Forward P/E ~35x on FY2026 consensus is premium but supported by 20%+ revenue CAGR and near-50% EBITDA margins. Beta of 0.48 is a structural risk reducer.
picks.exitTrigger: GLP-1 safety signal emerges from FDA post-market surveillance, or a rival drug (semaglutide combo or retatrutide) unexpectedly captures outsized market share, or CMS drug pricing negotiations impair insulin/GLP-1 economics
picks.keyRisk: FDA safety signal or unexpected competitor approval that materially disrupts the GLP-1 competitive moat

picks.name: Goldman Sachs Group Inc.
picks.rationale: GS is the premier capital markets and investment banking beneficiary of the current macro regime — steepened yield curve (+50bps 2s10s), re-accelerating bond issuance (high yield and investment grade both active in a volatile rate environment), and recovering M&A activity as deal conviction returns post-trade-war pause. Druckenmiller's Duquesne family office listed XLF (which is anchored by JPM, BRK.B, and GS) as its top buy in Q4 2025. Financials trade at a discount to the overall S&P 500 index, making GS one of the few growth-adjacent sectors not expensive. SPGI's Ratings revenue surprises to the upside when issuance is active — GS leads that issuance pipeline.
picks.catalyst: Q2 2026 earnings in mid-July, expected to show M&A advisory revenue acceleration and trading gains from elevated volatility
picks.entryNote: Financials sector broadly trades at a discount to the S&P 500 on forward earnings. GS benefits from the steepened 2s10s spread of +50bps — every 25bps of steepening is a meaningful NIM and trading revenue tailwind for bulge-bracket capital markets firms.
picks.exitTrigger: 2s10s spread re-inverts (signaling recession), credit markets seize up (HY OAS widens above 500bps), or regulatory capital increases under Basel III Endgame impose material revenue headwinds
picks.keyRisk: Credit cycle turning — a rapid expansion of HY OAS spreads from current ~279bps toward 500bps would signal default cycle risk that impairs GS's loan book and deal pipeline simultaneously

picks.name: Exxon Mobil Corporation
picks.rationale: XOM is the highest-quality energy name with direct exposure to the Strait of Hormuz supply shock and crude oil at ~$102/bbl — the sector is the deepest value in the market at ~32% discount to both the S&P 500 and its own 10-year average. XOM's Q1 2026 beat was driven by its trading desk outperformance and Pioneer acquisition synergies. As an integrated major, XOM has the lowest break-even cost structure, largest refining buffer, and highest FCF yield in the peer group. Energy institutional inflows are accelerating in 2026. Berkshire trimmed CVX modestly but XOM remains the peer with superior FCF generation at current crude prices.
picks.catalyst: Strait of Hormuz resolution (bullish for shares as risk premium partly unwinds; upside $165+ consensus) or continued supply disruption (supports $100+ crude and elevated FCF)
picks.catalystWindow: Ongoing through Q3 2026; next earnings report early August
picks.entryNote: Trading near $163 vs. analyst consensus target of ~$164 (MarketBeat). The stock has lagged the energy sector's YTD rally despite being the highest-quality name — offering a catch-up opportunity. Energy is the only sector at a 32% discount to both the index and its own history.
picks.exitTrigger: Crude oil price falls below $75/bbl on demand destruction (recession signal), or geopolitical resolution causes a rapid normalization of the oil risk premium below the fund's cost basis
picks.keyRisk: Global demand shock — if the US enters recession in H2 2026, crude demand collapses and the energy FCF thesis inverts rapidly

picks.name: RTX Corporation
picks.rationale: RTX is the premier defense and aerospace contractor with a diversified revenue mix across missiles (Raytheon), jet engines (Pratt & Whitney), and avionics (Collins Aerospace) — a structure that captures every dimension of the geopolitical upcycle driven by the Iran conflict, NATO rearmament, and Middle East escalation. Defense budgets are in a multi-year upcycle; XLI has returned 29.87% in the past year. RTX traded at $196.50 ahead of Q1 earnings and has been absorbing macro volatility well. It is a consistent top-10 holding in the portfolio this week and the thesis is firmly intact with no thesis-breaking developments. SCHD's LMT/RTX/defense weighting underscores the institutional comfort with defense names as inflation hedges.
picks.catalyst: Supplemental defense appropriations bill passage + Q2 2026 earnings in late July showing backlog expansion and Pratt & Whitney engine repair revenue acceleration
picks.catalystWindow: June-August 2026 (Congressional defense budget cycle and Q2 earnings)
picks.entryNote: Trading ~$196.50 near its 52-week highs, having recovered from early 2026 volatility. Forward P/E ~20-21x is fair for a business with a 5-year contracted backlog in the $200B+ range. The geopolitical premium is structural, not transient.
picks.exitTrigger: Iran ceasefire and comprehensive de-escalation causes defense budget supplemental approvals to be pulled, or Pratt & Whitney GTF engine inspection liability re-accelerates beyond reserved amounts
picks.keyRisk: Geopolitical de-escalation — a swift resolution to the Iran conflict removes the geopolitical risk premium and could trigger a sector rotation out of defense names

picks.name: S&P Global Inc.
picks.rationale: SPGI is a structural compounder with market cap of $119.56B that captures revenue from every dimension of the current macro environment — elevated bond issuance activity (Ratings), volatile commodities data demand (Commodity Insights/Platts), and the secular growth of index investing (S&P Dow Jones Indices). In the current regime with 30-year Treasury at 5.12% and corporate refinancing walls active, SPGI's Ratings segment historically surprises to the upside on issuance. The planned Mobility (Carfax) spin-off in 2026 is a near-term catalyst that should surface hidden value and improve segment clarity. It has been a consistent top-10 portfolio hold throughout this week.
picks.catalyst: Mobility segment spin-off execution in 2026 — a pure-play demerger that should unlock sum-of-the-parts value above the current consolidated trading multiple; next earnings in late July
picks.catalystWindow: H2 2026 for spin-off execution; Q2 2026 earnings in late July
picks.entryNote: Market cap $119.56B with analyst consensus target of $533.96 (1Y). Morningstar flags Ratings revenue as consistently outperforming on issuance cycles. Current forward P/E premium to the market is justified by the near-monopoly Ratings franchise and index AUM growth.
picks.exitTrigger: Bond issuance collapses (HY OAS >500bps, credit freeze), or DOJ antitrust challenge to the Ratings/Indices duopoly (S&P + Moody's), or the Mobility spin-off is cancelled or delayed past 2027
picks.keyRisk: Credit market seizure — if HY spreads blow out sharply, bond issuance dries up and Ratings revenue declines 15-25%, which is the segment with the highest incremental margins

picks.name: NVIDIA Corporation
picks.rationale: NVDA is the dominant AI infrastructure chip supplier with Q1 FY2027 earnings due May 20 — EPS expected +118.5% YoY and revenue +78.7% YoY, the most explosive growth in the Mag 7 cohort. GuruFocus assigns NVDA a GF Score of 100/100 with current price (~$230) seen as 28.9% undervalued vs. intrinsic value of $323.69. Today's NVDA range of $229.30-$236.54 (per Investing.com) shows the stock consolidating ahead of the May 20 print. The position is sized at medium conviction (7%) rather than high because of the export control overhang from the failed Trump-Xi summit and the documented 'sell the news' risk given perfection-priced expectations — but the structural AI capex cycle demands exposure.
picks.catalyst: Q1 FY2027 earnings May 20, 2026 — guidance for Blackwell ramp, data center revenue trajectory, and any commentary on H20/China export relief
picks.entryNote: NVDA at ~$232 intraday on May 15, within its 52-week range of $129.16-$236.54. Up 74.85% over the past year. The stock has re-rated significantly but GF Value model suggests 28.9% upside to intrinsic value at $323.69. Sized conservatively at 7% due to event risk.
picks.exitTrigger: May 20 earnings miss (revenue below $43B or guidance below Street for Q2), or new export controls on Blackwell chips to additional markets, or hyperscaler capex guidance cuts by MSFT/GOOG/AMZN
picks.keyRisk: Export control escalation on H20/Blackwell chips following the failed Trump-Xi summit — any new restriction is a direct revenue impairment for the fastest-growing segment

picks.name: Vistra Corp.
picks.rationale: VST is the stealth AI infrastructure play — a power generator directly levered to the exponential electricity demand from hyperscaler data centers, with EBITDA of $3.62B and a 25.25% EBITDA margin that has been expanding on AI data center load growth. Last quarter VST delivered EPS of $1.47 vs. $1.32 estimated (11.26% beat) and revenue of $5.64B vs. $5.22B estimated — a significant operational beat. The Meta partnership announced in January 2026 to supply power to AI data centers is a concrete revenue anchor. Next earnings August 6, 2026 with Q2 EPS estimated at $2.02. The stock has pulled back to ~$139 from $155-160 channel support — a constructive re-entry point. As Utilities tied to AI infrastructure are among the stealth performers per the momentum screen, VST offers differentiated sector exposure vs. the tech-heavy growth book.
picks.catalyst: Q2 2026 earnings August 6 showing AI data center load growth driving EBITDA beat; any announcement of additional hyperscaler power purchase agreements
picks.entryNote: VST at ~$139.21, having pulled back from the $155-160 channel support level. This creates a better risk/reward entry than chasing the prior highs. Analyst estimates revised upward for FY2026-2027 on AI power demand. EBITDA margin of 25.25% is expanding.
picks.exitTrigger: Hyperscaler data center build slows materially (AI capex bubble deflation tail risk), causing power purchase agreement demand to dry up; or Texas ERCOT regulatory changes impair VST's merchant power pricing
picks.keyRisk: AI capex bubble deflation — if hyperscalers reduce data center build-out, VST's contracted power demand thesis is undermined and the stock re-rates back toward a commodity utility multiple

picks.name: SPDR Gold Trust
picks.rationale: Gold at $4,625 is the clearest institutional safe-haven signal in the portfolio — 3-month ETF inflows remain strongly positive as of May 11, 2026. In a stagflationary regime where 30-year Treasury yields are above 5% yet gold is still rising, the underlying demand is genuine fear/inflation hedging, not simply rate-trade positioning. With PCE at 3.5% and re-accelerating, Iran/Hormuz still unresolved, and the dollar (DXY ~99.29) soft, every macro tailwind for gold is live simultaneously. The 5-10% portfolio hedge function is essential in this environment.
picks.catalyst: April PCE release (late May) re-accelerating above 3.7% — would be the single most bullish near-term catalyst for gold as rate hike probability crosses 50%
picks.catalystWindow: Late May 2026 (next 2 weeks) for PCE; ongoing geopolitical tail risk
picks.entryNote: Gold at $4,625 — historically elevated but in a confirmed uptrend with institutional flows backing the move. Not a valuation-driven entry; a macro hedge entry. Sized at 7% — meaningful but not a core equity bet.
picks.exitTrigger: Strait of Hormuz re-opens (removes geopolitical premium), AND inflation data falls sharply back toward 2% target, AND Fed cuts rates — the triple combination that removes all three legs of the gold bull case simultaneously
picks.keyRisk: Rapid geopolitical de-escalation and inflation normalization removing both the safe-haven and inflation-hedge bids simultaneously

picks.name: Schwab US Dividend Equity ETF
picks.rationale: SCHD is the best risk-off vehicle in the universe for a long-only fund — 3.39% dividend yield, 10.61% average annual dividend growth over 10 years, and only 9% tech exposure vs. 21% energy, 17% consumer defensive, and 16% healthcare. This composition is ideally suited for the current stagflation regime where tech faces multiple compression but energy/staples/healthcare outperform. SCHD held up better than broad market ETFs during 2022-style growth selloffs — exactly the scenario most probable if the 30-year Treasury sustains above 5%. Capital is rotating away from speculative AI stocks toward defensive dividend stocks per the sector rotation briefing.
picks.catalyst: Sustained equity market rotation from growth to value/income as the 30-year Treasury holds above 5% — SCHD is the structural beneficiary of that rotation
picks.entryNote: SCHD gained 12% YTD with a 3.39% dividend yield — offering income while waiting for volatility to resolve. At 3.4% yield vs. a 10-year at 4.59%, SCHD does not provide positive real carry vs. Treasuries, but its total return profile (dividend growth + capital appreciation) makes it superior to TLT for a multi-year hold.
picks.exitTrigger: Fed cuts aggressively and growth re-accelerates sharply — growth outperformance rotation would cause SCHD to lag meaningfully; sell and redeploy into growth names
picks.keyRisk: Dividend cuts within the underlying portfolio from energy names if crude collapses, or from financial names if credit spreads blow out

picks.name: Berkshire Hathaway Inc. Class B
picks.rationale: BRK.B is the ultimate quality compounder and cash fortress with $380B in cash/T-bills generating ~$16B/year in risk-free income at current rates. Greg Abel's first 13F filing (Q1 2026) showed dramatic portfolio simplification from 42 to 29 positions — a higher-conviction, less-diversified approach that signals confidence in the core holdings. The massive GOOGL add (+204%) and Delta Air Lines initiation signal technology acceptance and a bold cyclical recovery bet. In a drawdown, Berkshire deploys its cash opportunistically — it is the anti-fragile portfolio anchor. With operating businesses (GEICO, BNSF, Berkshire Energy) generating independent cash flows, BRK.B provides natural diversification across insurance, rail, and energy infrastructure.
picks.catalyst: Potential major acquisition announcement — the record $380B cash hoard signals a large acquisition is being hunted; any deployment at attractive prices would be an immediate re-rating catalyst
picks.entryNote: BRK.B at ~1.5x book — not historically cheap but reasonable given the T-bill income on the $380B cash pile. The cash itself is earning ~$16B/year at risk-free rates, providing a floor on intrinsic value that grows each quarter deals are not made.
picks.exitTrigger: Major acquisition at excessive premium that depletes the cash hoard without clear return on capital rationale, or Ted Weschler departure reduces investment management depth below Abel's capacity
picks.keyRisk: Greg Abel makes a large, poorly priced acquisition that destroys the cash fortress advantage — the primary risk in the post-Buffett transition era

picks.name: iShares 7-10 Year Treasury Bond ETF
picks.rationale: IEF provides intermediate-duration Treasury exposure — less rate-sensitive than TLT (which has 20+ year duration) while still providing genuine flight-to-quality ballast in a recession shock. The 10-year at 4.59% is at the 92nd percentile of its trailing range, meaning if growth surprises to the downside (GDP miss, ISM manufacturing contraction deepening), the 10-year yield falls and IEF appreciates — the classic recession hedge. Unlike TLT, IEF avoids the full brunt of fiscal premium expansion (term premium is widening most at the 20-30 year range). This is a tail-risk hedge, not a core return driver — sized at 5%.
picks.catalyst: Q2 GDP data or ISM Manufacturing print showing economic contraction; any Fed pivot signal from Kevin Warsh would send intermediate rates sharply lower and IEF higher
picks.catalystWindow: June-August 2026 (Q2 data releases and FOMC meetings)
picks.entryNote: IEF at ~4.59% yield on the 10-year — the highest since February 2025. Entering at historically elevated rates (92nd percentile) means the asymmetry favors appreciation if growth disappoints. Duration risk is meaningful if rates continue rising, hence the conservative 5% weight.
picks.exitTrigger: 10-year Treasury yield breaks above 5.25% on continued inflation re-acceleration and term premium expansion — at that level, the recession hedge function fails and IEF should be reduced
picks.keyRisk: Fiscal premium spiral — the 'Big Beautiful Bill' passing with unfunded tax cuts causes the 10-year to breach 5.25%, making IEF a losing position even in a growth slowdown

picks.name: The Coca-Cola Company
picks.rationale: KO is the highest-quality consumer staples compounder with unmatched global brand pricing power, a ~3.1% dividend yield, and near-zero correlation to AI capex cycles or semiconductor export controls. In a stagflationary environment, Coca-Cola's ability to pass through inflation via pricing (demonstrated in 2022-2024) is a structural advantage. Consumer Staples is up 15% YTD, outperforming the flat broader market, and institutional allocators have been considering overweights to the sector. KO provides the staples exposure in the defensive sleeve that complements GLD (commodity hedge), SCHD (dividend equity), BRK.B (quality compounder), and IEF (duration hedge) — completing the five-distinct-exposure requirement.
picks.catalyst: Q2 2026 earnings in late July showing continued pricing power and volume recovery in international markets (Latin America, Asia-Pacific) as dollar softness boosts reported results
picks.entryNote: KO trades at ~20x forward P/E — slightly above its 10-year median of ~19x, but the premium is justified by the combination of defensive earnings quality and 3%+ dividend yield. The DXY at ~99.29 (weak dollar) is a tailwind for KO's international revenue translation.
picks.exitTrigger: Volume declines in core markets despite pricing (elasticity finally breaking), or a sharp reversal in the DXY above 105 that crushes international revenue translation; or dividend cut
picks.keyRisk: Consumer trade-down accelerating — if the K-shaped economy's lower cohort reduces discretionary beverage spending, KO's volume growth stalls even as pricing sustains nominal revenue

summary: The portfolio is positioned for a late-cycle stagflationary stress regime — not a clean soft landing — where nominal growth (GDPNow Q2 at 3.7%) coexists with re-accelerating inflation (PCE 3.5%, CPI 3.8%), a new Fed chair, and long rates at fresh cycle highs (30-year at 5.12%). The 10 growth picks express conviction in the secular winners of this regime: AI infrastructure and cloud (GOOGL, AVGO, META, NVDA), inflation-adjacent value (XOM), rate-cycle beneficiaries (GS, SPGI), defensive secular growth (LLY), geopolitical premium (RTX), and AI power infrastructure (VST) — collectively spanning Communication Services, Information Technology, Energy, Financials, Health Care, Industrials, and Utilities across at least 6 distinct GICS sectors. All seven core growth carries (GOOGL, AVGO, META, LLY, GS, XOM, RTX, SPGI) are thesis-intact carry-overs from prior sessions this week; NVDA returns at medium conviction ahead of its May 20 binary earnings event, and VST is maintained at medium conviction given its pullback to channel support at $139 providing a better entry. The dominant risk being underwritten is an AI capex bubble deflation event or a fiscal premium spiral pushing the 30-year above 5.5%, which would simultaneously compress growth multiples and impair duration assets — the GLD, SCHD, and BRK.B defensive positions are specifically sized to absorb that scenario.
diversificationNote: The growth book spans 6 GICS sectors: Communication Services (GOOGL, META — two names sharing digital advertising but differentiated by cloud vs. social AI monetization), Information Technology (AVGO, NVDA — complementary points in the AI value chain: custom ASIC/networking vs. GPU training), Financials (GS, SPGI — capital markets revenue and data/Ratings duopoly), Health Care (LLY — GLP-1 secular growth), Energy (XOM — integrated major FCF), Industrials (RTX — defense), and Utilities (VST — AI power infrastructure); the defensive sleeve spans gold (GLD), broad dividend-quality equity (SCHD), quality compounder/cash fortress (BRK.B), intermediate-duration Treasuries (IEF), and consumer staples (KO) — five distinct exposures with no duplication.

phaseData.macro:
Here is your macro strategy briefing for Friday, May 15, 2026:

---

## 📊 US EQUITIES MACRO BRIEFING — May 15, 2026

### DATA DASHBOARD

**Fed Policy**
The effective Fed Funds rate currently sits at ~4.25–4.50% (unchanged since the last meeting). 
Treasury yields spiked sharply Friday as traders priced in interest rate policy under newly confirmed Fed Chair Kevin Warsh, who was confirmed by the Senate on Wednesday.
 
The March 2026 dot plot projected a 3.00–3.25% Fed funds rate by year-end 2027.
 However, 
markets have fully priced out any possibility of a Fed rate cut this year, while some traders are increasingly betting on a potential rate hike by December.
 
Per CME FedWatch, traders see no rate cuts in 2026 at all — reversing expectations from months ago — with Bank of America extending its first cut forecast to the second half of 2027, and JPMorgan keeping CPI above 3.0% until early 2027.


**Yield Curve**

The 10-year note finished May 15, 2026 at 4.59% and the 2-year at 4.09%, marking the highest level for each since February 2025.
 
The 30-year bond jumped nearly 11 basis points to 5.121%, the highest since May 22, 2025, and nearing the highest since October 2023.
 The 2s10s spread is a **+50 bps** positive steepener, and the 3m10y is also positive — a curve that has re-steepened sharply from prolonged inversion. Term premium is clearly widening, a bearish signal for long-duration assets.

**Inflation — RE-ACCELERATING** 🔴

In April, the CPI for all items rose 0.6% month-over-month (SA) and 3.8% over the last 12 months; core CPI (all items less food and energy) rose 0.4% MoM and 2.8% YoY.
 
PCE inflation jumped to 3.50% in March from 2.80% in February 2026.
 
Both the CPI and PPI reports released this week suggest the energy shock is pushing US inflation higher.
 
March CPI surged to 3.3% YoY — the highest since May 2024 — driven by an energy shock that pushed gasoline prices up 18.9% YoY.
 
Mounting inflationary pressures tied to the Iran war drove the 10-year above 4.5%; wholesale inflation accelerated at its fastest pace since 2022 in April.
 Supercore (core services ex-shelter) is sticky and elevated given labor market resilience.

**Growth**

The Atlanta Fed GDPNow model estimate for real GDP growth (SAAR) in Q2 2026 is 3.7% as of May 8.
 
The BEA's first estimate of Q1 real GDP growth was 2.0%, 0.8 percentage points above GDPNow's final nowcast.
 
Retail sales growth slowed in line with expectations but continued to reflect resilient consumer spending.
 Growth is holding, but the Q1 miss vs. nowcast bears watching. ISM Manufacturing re-entered contraction territory in early May per recent data, while ISM Services remains in modest expansion — a classic late-cycle bifurcation.

**Credit & Liquidity**
The DXY is at ~99.29 (slightly off its lows). HY OAS spreads have widened modestly from their tightest levels of the year (~310–330 bps, directionally wider), and IG OAS remains tight (~90–95 bps) — the market has not yet entered a meaningful risk-off repricing, but the direction is a warning. Financial conditions (Goldman FCI, Chicago Fed NFCI) remain mildly accommodative relative to history, but are tightening at the margin as long rates spike.

**Earnings Backdrop**

The S&P 500 closed at 7,398.93 on Friday May 8, marking its sixth straight weekly gain and the longest streak since 2024.
 Today's session shows the index at ~7,432 before the afternoon selloff. The S&P 500 forward P/E sits near 21–22x on 2026 consensus EPS — a premium to the 10-year average of ~18x — compressing the equity risk premium to near-zero given a 10-year at 4.59%. 
The March 2026 SEP showed a 30-basis-point increase in year-end 2026 PCE inflation (to 2.7%) and a 10-basis-point increase in 2026 real GDP growth (to 2.4%).


**Geopolitical / Event Tape**

The situation in the Middle East remains fragile and far from resolved, with Trump describing Iran's latest proposal as "unacceptable," while oil prices continue to climb amid fears of disruptions to global supply, with the Strait of Hormuz still closed.
 
A summit between Trump and Chinese President Xi ended without major agreements, including no indication that Beijing would help resolve the conflict.


---

### ¶1 — REGIME CALL: **LATE-CYCLE / TRANSITIONING TO STAGFLATIONARY STRESS**

This is a **late-cycle regime with stagflationary overtones** — not a clean soft landing. You have nominal growth that is holding (Atlanta GDPNow Q2 at 3.7%), but inflation is re-accelerating hard above 3.5% on the back of an energy/geopolitical shock (Iran/Hormuz), a new Fed chair inheriting a nearly impossible mandate, markets fully pricing out cuts, and long-end yields surging to fresh cycle highs (30y at 5.12%). The real policy rate is firmly restrictive (Fed funds ~4.35% minus headline PCE ~3.5% = ~+85 bps real), but the bond market is demanding even more compensation at the long end as term premium expands. The equity risk premium is near zero — a condition historically associated with sub-par forward equity returns.

---

### ¶2 — WHAT'S WORKING / WHAT'S BREAKING

**Working:** Energy and commodity-linked equities (direct beneficiary of the Hormuz/oil shock — XLE, OXY, CVX); short-duration value — Financials (steeper yield curve benefits net interest margin for banks; XLF outperforming); Defense & Aerospace (geopolitical premium; RTX, LMT, NOC); Healthcare (defensive earnings, relatively rate-insensitive, shelter in a stagflation storm). Real assets and inflation-linked instruments (TIPS, gold proxies, infrastructure) are also outperforming. Within the broad market, 
resilient consumer spending
 continues to support Consumer Staples selectively.

**Breaking:** Long-duration growth — mega-cap Tech and AI names whose discounted cash flow models are acutely sensitive to discount rate expansion; 
the 10-year surging to 4.595% while the 2-year moved to 4.079%
 is a violent headwind for high-multiple Nasdaq constituents. Small caps (Russell 2000) are being squeezed by higher-for-longer refinancing risk. Real estate (XLRE) is structurally impaired at 5%+ long rates. The forward P/E compression trade is live — any multiple contraction from 21–22x back toward the 10-year mean of ~18x implies ~15% downside to index levels independent of earnings risk.

---

### ¶3 — WHAT TO WATCH (Highest-Impact, 1–6 Month Horizon)

1. **April PCE (late May) + May CPI (June 10):** 
The Consumer Price Index for May 2026 is scheduled for release on June 10, 2026.
 
Investors will quickly pivot to April PCE (late May), the Fed's preferred measure, alongside May non-farm payrolls and retail sales.
 If PCE re-accelerates above 3.7–3.8%, the hike probability in futures could cross 50% — a potential shock to equity multiple assumptions across the board.

2. **Iran Nuclear Negotiations / Strait of Hormuz:** 
The Middle East remains unresolved, with the Strait of Hormuz still closed and oil prices climbing on supply disruption fears.
 A re-opening of the Strait would sharply disinflationary — cutting 50–80 bps off headline CPI trajectory — and could be the single most bullish macro event for equities over the next 6 months. Escalation, conversely, is the tail risk that breaks the cycle into outright contraction.

3. **New Fed Chair Kevin Warsh's First Policy Signal / June FOMC:** 
Yields spiked Friday as traders looked to price interest rate policy under new Fed Chair Kevin Warsh.
 
While Warsh is viewed as more open to rate cuts, the latest FOMC meeting showed three voters ready to dissent over policy-statement language — suggesting a rate cut still looks unlikely without significantly softer inflation data.
 His first public communication and any June FOMC statement language adjustment will be the highest-information-content macro event for rate-sensitive assets in Q3.

---
> *All data as of market close or intraday May 15, 2026. This briefing is for informational purposes and does not constitute investment advice.*

**Inflation — RE-ACCELERATING** 🔴

In April, the CPI for all items rose 0.6% month-over-month (SA) and 3.8% over the last 12 months; core CPI (all items less food and energy) rose 0.4% MoM and 2.8% YoY.
 
PCE inflation jumped to 3.50% in March from 2.80% in February 2026.
 
Both the CPI and PPI reports released this week suggest the energy shock is pushing US inflation higher.
 
March CPI surged to 3.3% YoY — the highest since May 2024 — driven by an energy shock that pushed gasoline prices up 18.9% YoY.
 
Mounting inflationary pressures tied to the Iran war drove the 10-year above 4.5%; wholesale inflation accelerated at its fastest pace since 2022 in April.
 Supercore (core services ex-shelter) is sticky and elevated given labor market resilience.

**Growth**

The Atlanta Fed GDPNow model estimate for real GDP growth (SAAR) in Q2 2026 is 3.7% as of May 8.
 
The BEA's first estimate of Q1 real GDP growth was 2.0%, 0.8 percentage points above GDPNow's final nowcast.

 Growth is holding, but the Q1 miss vs. nowcast bears watching. ISM Manufacturing re-entered contraction territory in early May per recent data, while ISM Services remains in modest expansion — a classic late-cycle bifurcation.

**Credit & Liquidity**
The DXY is at ~99.29 (slightly off its lows). HY OAS spreads have widened modestly from their tightest levels of the year (~310–330 bps, directionally wider), and IG OAS remains tight (~90–95 bps) — the market has not yet entered a meaningful risk-off repricing, but the direction is a warning. Financial conditions (Goldman FCI, Chicago Fed NFCI) remain mildly accommodative relative to history, but are tightening at the margin as long rates spike.

**Earnings Backdrop**

The S&P 500 closed at 7,398.93 on Friday May 8, marking its sixth straight weekly gain and the longest streak since 2024.
 Today's session shows the index at ~7,432 before the afternoon selloff. The S&P 500 forward P/E sits near 21–22x on 2026 consensus EPS — a premium to the 10-year average of ~18x — compressing the equity risk premium to near-zero given a 10-year at 4.59%.

**Geopolitical / Event Tape**

The situation in the Middle East remains fragile and far from resolved, with Trump describing Iran's latest proposal as "unacceptable," while oil prices continue to climb amid fears of disruptions to global supply, with the Strait of Hormuz still closed.
 
A summit between Trump and Chinese President Xi ended without major agreements, including no indication that Beijing would help resolve the conflict.


---

### ¶1 — REGIME CALL: **LATE-CYCLE / TRANSITIONING TO STAGFLATIONARY STRESS**

This is a **late-cycle regime with stagflationary overtones** — not a clean soft landing. You have nominal growth that is holding (Atlanta GDPNow Q2 at 3.7%), but inflation is re-accelerating hard above 3.5% on the back of an energy/geopolitical shock (Iran/Hormuz), a new Fed chair inheriting a nearly impossible mandate, markets fully pricing out cuts, and long-end yields surging to fresh cycle highs (30y at 5.12%). The real policy rate is firmly restrictive (Fed funds ~4.35% minus headline PCE ~3.5% = ~+85 bps real), but the bond market is demanding even more compensation at the long end as term premium expands. The Consumer Price Index for May 2026 is scheduled for release on June 10, 2026.
